Therefore, one might ask the question whether every irreducible facet defining Steiner cut inequality for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G, T)\) in fact is valid (and thus facet defining) for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\). Again, Theorem 6 at least shows that this holds for \(|T| \le 5\).
title: Characterize when irreducible Steiner-cut facets remain valid for global cut dominants
background_latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a connected undirected graph and let \(T\subseteq V\) be a terminal set with \(|T|\ge 2\). For \(S\subseteq V\), write \(\delta(S)\subseteq E\) for the cut consisting of edges with one endpoint in \(S\) and the other in \(V\setminus S\). A \(T\)-Steiner cut is a cut \(\delta(S)\) such that \(T\cap S\neq\emptyset\) and \(T\setminus S\neq\emptyset\).

Let \(\chi(\delta(S))\in\{0,1\}^E\) denote the incidence vector of \(\delta(S)\). The \(T\)-Steiner cut polytope is
\[\mathrm{CUT}(G,T)=\mathrm{conv}\{\chi(\delta(S)) : \delta(S)\text{ is a }T\text{-Steiner cut}\}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^E.\]
The \(T\)-Steiner cut dominant is the dominant (upper-closed hull)
\[\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)=\mathrm{CUT}(G,T)+\mathbb{R}^E_{\ge 0} = \{y\in\mathbb{R}^E: \exists x\in \mathrm{CUT}(G,T)\text{ with }y\ge x\}.\]
Similarly, the (global) cut dominant is \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)=\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,V)\).

Consider a nontrivial valid inequality for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) of the form
\[c\cdot x \ge \gamma,\]
where \(c\in\mathbb{R}^E_{\ge 0}\) and \(\gamma>0\). Such an inequality is facet-defining for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) if the face \(\{x\in \mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T): c\cdot x=\gamma\}\) has dimension \(|E|-1\). The paper uses the notion of an irreducible facet inducing Steiner graph \((G,T)\), meaning (i) \(G\) has no cut vertex, and (ii) no nonterminal node \(v\in V\setminus T\) has degree 2. (Equivalently, such a facet is not obtainable from smaller facet-inducing instances by the paper’s subdivision and gluing operations.)

A key monotonicity relation is \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\subseteq \mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) whenever \(T\subseteq V\): any inequality valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) is automatically valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\), but the converse may fail. The paper notes explicit examples where enlarging the terminal set (turning a nonterminal into a terminal) can destroy validity of a previously facet-defining Steiner-cut inequality.
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following statement is true or false.

For every connected graph \(G=(V,E)\), every terminal set \(T\subseteq V\) with \(|T|\ge 2\), and every inequality \(c\cdot x\ge \gamma\) that defines a facet of the Steiner cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) and is irreducible in the sense that \((G,T)\) is an irreducible facet inducing Steiner graph, the same inequality is valid for the global cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)=\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,V)\).

Equivalently: characterize whether every facet-defining inequality of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) coming from an irreducible facet-inducing pair \((G,T)\) remains valid (and hence facet-defining) after replacing \(T\) by \(V\).
latex:
Let \(G=(V,E)\) be a connected undirected graph and let \(T\subseteq V\) be a terminal set with \(|T|\ge 2\). For \(S\subseteq V\), write \(\delta(S)\subseteq E\) for the cut consisting of edges with one endpoint in \(S\) and the other in \(V\setminus S\). A \(T\)-Steiner cut is a cut \(\delta(S)\) such that \(T\cap S\neq\emptyset\) and \(T\setminus S\neq\emptyset\).

Let \(\chi(\delta(S))\in\{0,1\}^E\) denote the incidence vector of \(\delta(S)\). The \(T\)-Steiner cut polytope is
\[\mathrm{CUT}(G,T)=\mathrm{conv}\{\chi(\delta(S)) : \delta(S)\text{ is a }T\text{-Steiner cut}\}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^E.\]
The \(T\)-Steiner cut dominant is the dominant (upper-closed hull)
\[\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)=\mathrm{CUT}(G,T)+\mathbb{R}^E_{\ge 0} = \{y\in\mathbb{R}^E: \exists x\in \mathrm{CUT}(G,T)\text{ with }y\ge x\}.\]
Similarly, the (global) cut dominant is \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)=\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,V)\).

Consider a nontrivial valid inequality for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) of the form
\[c\cdot x \ge \gamma,\]
where \(c\in\mathbb{R}^E_{\ge 0}\) and \(\gamma>0\). Such an inequality is facet-defining for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) if the face \(\{x\in \mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T): c\cdot x=\gamma\}\) has dimension \(|E|-1\). The paper uses the notion of an irreducible facet inducing Steiner graph \((G,T)\), meaning (i) \(G\) has no cut vertex, and (ii) no nonterminal node \(v\in V\setminus T\) has degree 2. (Equivalently, such a facet is not obtainable from smaller facet-inducing instances by the paper’s subdivision and gluing operations.)

A key monotonicity relation is \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\subseteq \mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) whenever \(T\subseteq V\): any inequality valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) is automatically valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\), but the converse may fail. The paper notes explicit examples where enlarging the terminal set (turning a nonterminal into a terminal) can destroy validity of a previously facet-defining Steiner-cut inequality.

Determine whether the following statement is true or false.

For every connected graph \(G=(V,E)\), every terminal set \(T\subseteq V\) with \(|T|\ge 2\), and every inequality \(c\cdot x\ge \gamma\) that defines a facet of the Steiner cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) and is irreducible in the sense that \((G,T)\) is an irreducible facet inducing Steiner graph, the same inequality is valid for the global cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)=\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,V)\).

Equivalently: characterize whether every facet-defining inequality of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) coming from an irreducible facet-inducing pair \((G,T)\) remains valid (and hence facet-defining) after replacing \(T\) by \(V\).
location_in_paper: Section 9 (Conclusion), paragraph titled 'Upwards validity', page 22 (of 24 in the provided text)
open_problem_quote: Therefore, one might ask the question whether every irreducible facet defining Steiner cut inequality for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G, T)\) in fact is valid (and thus facet defining) for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\). Again, Theorem 6 at least shows that this holds for \(|T| \le 5\).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existentially refutable: one can enumerate small irreducible facet-inducing pairs \((G,T)\), compute facets of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) via vertex enumeration/separation on the finite cut set, and then check validity against \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) by optimizing \(c\cdot x\) over all cuts. This makes automated small-instance counterexample search plausible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexamples can be represented as small graphs \(G\) with terminal sets \(T\), and a fitness signal can be based on (i) finding an irreducible facet of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) computationally and (ii) measuring its minimum slack over \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\). The bottleneck is reliably identifying facets for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) (a polyhedral computation) which is feasible only for modest sizes, suggesting medium suitability.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The upward-validity question asks whether every facet-defining inequality \(c\cdot x\ge \gamma\) of a Steiner cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) that is irreducible (no cut vertex in \(G\), and no degree-2 nonterminal) must remain valid for the global cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)=\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,V)\). In the source paper, Conforti and Kaibel develop a detailed structural theory for facets of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\): every facet admits a laminar root basis (an uncrossing/laminarity phenomenon), and every facet-inducing instance can be built from finitely many irreducible ones via subdivision and gluing. They prove a complete classification for \(|T|\le 5\), showing that all facets come from Steiner tree and Steiner cactus inequalities; in particular, irreducible facets are just terminal cycles, and these inequalities are valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\), establishing upward validity in this range.

Beyond five terminals, the paper exhibits that enlarging the terminal set can destroy validity, but the known counterexamples rely on turning a degree-2 nonterminal into a terminal. This leaves open whether irreducibility suffices to prevent such failures. A key constraint comes from Conforti–Fiorini–Pashkovich’s forbidden-minor theorem for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\): if an inequality with right-hand side \(\gamma>2\) were globally facet-defining, then \(G\) must contain a prism or pyramid minor. The source paper uses this to rule out small irreducible non-cactus candidates when \(|T|\le 5\), but for \(|T|\ge 6\) computer enumeration already finds irreducible facet-inducing Steiner graphs with \(\gamma=4\), including the prism itself, suggesting that any resolution will likely require understanding how such higher-\(\gamma\) Steiner facets interact with non-Steiner cuts.

At present there is no forward-citation evidence provided here of a resolution, and the source paper explicitly lists the irreducible-upward-validity question as open. Promising directions include (i) searching for a counterexample among the enumerated irreducible facet-inducing Steiner graphs with six terminals by explicitly checking validity against \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) (e.g., via separation/optimization over compact formulations), and (ii) proving a general “upward closure” theorem for irreducible instances using laminar root bases and minor-based obstructions, potentially showing that any violation forces a reducible configuration (such as a degree-2 nonterminal) or a specific minor structure.

sources.title: Steiner Cut Dominants
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Steiner cut dominants \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\), proves existence of laminar root bases for facets, develops subdivision and gluing operations, and classifies all facet-defining inequalities for \(|T|\le 5\) as Steiner tree and Steiner cactus inequalities. Poses the open question of whether every irreducible facet-defining Steiner-cut inequality remains valid for the global cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\).
sources.connection: This is the source paper formulating the upward-validity question: whether irreducible facets of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,T)\) remain valid for \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\). It proves the statement for \(|T|\le 5\) and provides counterexamples to upward validity when a degree-2 nonterminal is turned into a terminal, leaving the irreducible case open.

sources.title: Cut dominants and forbidden minors
sources.bibtex:
@article{ConfortiFioriniPashkovich2016CutDominantsForbiddenMinors,
  title   = {Cut dominants and forbidden minors},
  author  = {Conforti, Michele and Fiorini, Samuel and Pashkovich, Kanstantsin},
  journal = {SIAM Journal on Discrete Mathematics},
  volume  = {30},
  number  = {3},
  pages   = {1571--1589},
  year    = {2016},
  doi     = {10.1137/15M1010555},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1137/15M1010555}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives a forbidden-minor characterization of graphs whose cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) admits a description by inequalities with right-hand side at most 2 (in minimum integer form). In particular, any facet of \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G)\) with \(\gamma>2\) forces a prism or pyramid minor.
sources.connection: Provides the main obstruction tool used in the source paper: if an irreducible Steiner facet were also valid globally with \(\gamma>2\), then \(G\) must contain a prism/pyramid minor. This constrains possible counterexamples and is used to prove the \(|T|\le 5\) upward-validity result.

sources.title: On the dominant of the s-t-cut polytope: vertices, facets, and adjacency
sources.bibtex:
@article{SkutellaWeber2010DominantSTCutPolytope,
  title   = {On the dominant of the s-t-cut polytope: vertices, facets, and adjacency},
  author  = {Skutella, Martin and Weber, Alexia},
  journal = {Mathematical Programming},
  volume  = {124},
  number  = {1-2},
  pages   = {441--454},
  year    = {2010},
  doi     = {10.1007/s10107-010-0373-7},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1007/s10107-010-0373-7}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Studies the \(s\)-\(t\) cut dominant \(\mathrm{CUT}^+(G,\{s,t\})\): characterizes vertices/facets and adjacency structure, relating facets to \(s\)-\(t\) path inequalities and related combinatorial objects.
sources.connection: Serves as the base case \(|T|=2\) where upward validity holds (path inequalities are valid for global cut dominant). Provides techniques and intuition for dominant polyhedra facets that motivate the Steiner generalization.

sources.title: The traveling salesman problem on a graph and some related integer polyhedra
sources.bibtex:
@article{CornuejolsFonluptNaddef1985TSPGraphRelatedPolyhedra,
  title   = {The traveling salesman problem on a graph and some related integer polyhedra},
  author  = {Cornu{\'e}jols, G{\'e}rard and Fonlupt, Jean and Naddef, Denis},
  journal = {Mathematical Programming},
  volume  = {33},
  number  = {1},
  pages   = {1--27},
  year    = {1985},
  doi     = {10.1007/BF01582008},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1007/BF01582008}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops polyhedral results for TSP on a graph and related blocking polyhedra; includes laminarity/uncrossing-type structural results that underlie facet descriptions via blocking duality.
sources.connection: The source paper’s laminar root-basis theorem is presented as a generalization of a corresponding statement for cut dominants obtainable via blocking duality from results in this line of work.

sources.title: Compacting cuts: a new linear formulation for minimum cut
sources.bibtex:
@article{CarrKonjevodLittleNatarajanParekh2009CompactingCuts,
  title   = {Compacting cuts: a new linear formulation for minimum cut},
  author  = {Carr, Robert D. and Konjevod, Goran and Little, Andrew and Natarajan, Vivek and Parekh, Anupam},
  journal = {ACM Transactions on Algorithms},
  volume  = {5},
  number  = {3},
  pages   = {27:1--27:16},
  year    = {2009},
  doi     = {10.1145/1541885.1541888},
  url     = {https://doi.org/10.1145/1541885.1541888}
}
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces a compact higher-dimensional linear formulation whose projection’s dominant equals the cut dominant, enabling minimum cut optimization via LP over a compact relaxation.
sources.connection: While not addressing facet validity, it provides an alternative polyhedral representation of cut dominants that could be used to test validity of candidate inequalities or search for counterexamples computationally.
